seo.description: Practice hiring-critical hash map interview questions including frequency count, first non-repeating character, two sum, valid anagram, and group anagrams.

questions.title: Character Frequency Count
questions.reference_note: Common interview warm-up used in many screening rounds.
questions.prompt:
Given a string, count the frequency of each character.

Rules:
- Treat uppercase and lowercase as different characters
- Include digits and symbols if present
- Return a dictionary where keys are characters and values are counts

This is one of the most important starter problems for mastering dictionaries.

questions.title: First Non-Repeating Character
questions.reference_note: Appears in multiple company prep lists including Goldman Sachs question sets.
questions.prompt:
Given a string, find the first character that appears exactly once.

Return that character.
If no such character exists, return None.

This is a very common hiring problem because it tests both counting and ordered scanning.

Second pass: scan the original string.
Return the first character whose count is 1.
If none found, return None.

questions.title: Two Sum
questions.reference_note: Classic LeetCode problem. Part of Blind-75 interview prep list.
questions.prompt:
Given an array of integers and a target value, return the indices of two numbers whose sum equals the target.

Rules:
- Return indices as a tuple (i, j)
- If no pair exists, return None
- You may assume at most one valid answer

This is one of the most famous hiring problems based on hash maps.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 820 300" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two Sum with complement lookup"><defs><linearGradient id="hm3bg" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="1"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#ff9f1c" stop-opacity="0.18"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#ffb74d" stop-opacity="0.08"/></linearGradient></defs><rect x="0" y="0" width="820" height="300" rx="22" fill="url(#hm3bg)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="24" y="34" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Two Sum</text><text x="24" y="58" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">For each number x, compute needed = target - x and look it up in a dictionary</text><rect x="24" y="82" width="772" height="62" rx="16" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.92)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.12)"/><text x="42" y="106" font-size="12" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9</text><g font-size="15" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328"><rect x="42" y="114" width="60" height="18" rx="6" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="72" y="127" text-anchor="middle">2</text><rect x="110" y="114" width="60" height="18" rx="6" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.30)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.14)"/><text x="140" y="127" text-anchor="middle">7</text><rect x="178" y="114" width="60" height="18" rx="6" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.12)"/><text x="208" y="127" text-anchor="middle">11</text><rect x="246" y="114" width="60" height="18" rx="6" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.12)"/><text x="276" y="127" text-anchor="middle">15</text></g><rect x="24" y="166" width="248" height="110" rx="16" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.92)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.12)"/><text x="42" y="194" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Step at index 1</text><text x="42" y="220" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Current value = 7</text><text x="42" y="242" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">needed = 9 - 7 = 2</text><text x="42" y="264" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Is 2 already seen?</text><path d="M 292 220 L 374 220" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/><path d="M 364 212 L 374 220 L 364 228" fill="none" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/><text x="305" y="208" font-size="12" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">lookup</text><rect x="392" y="166" width="404" height="110" rx="16" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.92)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.12)"/><text x="412" y="194" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Seen Dictionary</text><rect x="412" y="208" width="160" height="42" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.22)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.12)"/><text x="492" y="234" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">2 : 0</text><text x="596" y="232" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">needed value found</text><rect x="644" y="208" width="132" height="42" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.98)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.12)"/><text x="710" y="234" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">(0, 1)</text></svg>
hints: Use a dictionary to store numbers you have already seen.
For each number x, compute needed = target - x.
If needed is already in dictionary, return its index and current index.
Otherwise store current number and index.

print(two_sum([3, 2, 4], 6))

questions.title: Valid Anagram
questions.reference_note: Frequently seen in interview prep lists and screening practice.
questions.prompt:
Given two strings s and t, determine whether t is an anagram of s.

An anagram uses the same characters with the same frequencies.

Return True if they are anagrams, otherwise False.

This is a classic interview problem because it tests counting and comparison.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 820 290" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Valid anagram by comparing character counts"><defs><linearGradient font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Two strings are anagrams if every character has the same frequency</text><rect x="24" y="82" width="350" height="176" rx="18" Build frequency count for first string.
Build frequency count for second string.
Compare both dictionaries.

questions.title: Group Anagrams
questions.reference_note: Common interview question featured in major interview prep tracks.
questions.prompt:
Given a list of words, group the anagrams together.

Return a list of groups.
Words belong to the same group if they contain the same letters with the same frequencies.

This is a high-value interview problem because it combines hashing, canonical representation, and grouping.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 840 320" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Group anagrams using canonical key"><defs><linearGradient id="hm5bg" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Build a canonical key for each word, then group words with the same key</text><rect x="24" y="82" width="246" height="214" rx="18" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.92)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.12)"/><text x="42" y="110" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Words</text><g font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328"><rect x="42" y="124" width="88"
